one_line_takeaway: A tutorial survey mapping security and privacy threats and countermeasures across 6G's converging communication, sensing, and computing subsystems, contributing an organizing taxonomy and cross-cutting design principles rather than new experimental or theoretical results.
key_finding: The paper does not report an empirical or theoretical finding of its own (e.g., no new theorem, no measured attack success rate, no defense evaluation). Its main analytical takeaway is architectural: as 6G integrates communication, sensing, and AI-driven computing into one system, security risks that were previously siloed per-subsystem (eavesdropping in communication, spoofing/jamming in sensing, data leakage/Byzantine faults in distributed computing) become interdependent, and cryptographic techniques alone (e.g., end-to-end encryption, homomorphic encryption) are argued to be insufficient or impractical for several of these settings -- physical-layer/information-theoretic techniques (PLS, PLKG, secure coded computing, differential privacy via channel noise) are positioned as necessary complements. The paper argues DeepJSCC/semantic-communication schemes introduce a structural new vulnerability: because channel input is a direct (often analog) function of the source signal, some information about the input is always recoverable by an eavesdropper ("graceful degradation" also being a leakage channel), and conventional discrete-alphabet encryption cannot be directly applied.
core_contribution: This is a tutorial/survey paper (not a paper reporting new experiments) that synthesizes security and privacy research across three converging 6G functionalities -- communication, sensing, and computing -- and proposes an organizing taxonomy plus a set of cross-cutting design principles for a 'unified security framework' spanning them. Its own original contribution is the survey structure and synthesis itself: (1) a review of physical-layer security (wiretap channel, covert/LPD communication, physical-layer key generation, physical-layer authentication, quantum key distribution) and of security/privacy issues in semantic and pragmatic (goal-oriented) communications, including a walkthrough of the complexity-leakage-utility bottleneck (CLUB) framework and DeepJSCC-based approaches; (2) a taxonomy of attacks on wireless sensing decomposed into attacks on signal sources (jamming, spoofing, source hijacking), sensing channels (RIS-based manipulation, relay-based injection, passive-reflector placement), and sensing targets (EM stealth, EM spoofing, EM leakage), each paired with corresponding defenses; (3) a survey of secure coded computing techniques (coded distributed computing for straggler/secrecy tolerance, secure aggregation and differential privacy for federated learning, function-computation privacy); and (4) a section on adversarial ML attacks against wireless-network ML models, reformulating classical adversarial-attack taxonomies (white/gray/black-box, evasion/poisoning/Trojan/inference, best-effort vs. minimum-norm formulations) as a general optimization-based threat description. The paper ends with a set of open research directions (quantum-resistant algorithms, low-complexity privacy-preserving computation, adversarial robustness for AI-enabled networks, biometric/BCI privacy, usable security).
threat_model: No single fixed threat model -- the paper surveys many threat models drawn from the literature it reviews, organized by subsystem: passive eavesdroppers and covert-communication wardens for physical-layer security; honest-but-curious, malicious, and colluding worker nodes for secure coded computing; jamming/spoofing/hijacking adversaries and RIS/relay/passive-reflector manipulators for sensing; and white/gray/black-box adversaries mounting evasion, poisoning, Trojan, or inference attacks against ML models embedded in wireless systems. The paper itself does not adopt or analyze a single novel threat model as its own contribution; it catalogs and reorganizes threat models already defined in the cited primary-research literature.
limitations: As a survey, it inherits whatever limitations exist in the primary sources it summarizes and does not itself validate, replicate, or quantitatively compare the surveyed techniques. It provides no comparative benchmarking of the defenses it lists (e.g., no measured detection rates for the IRS/relay/passive-reflector sensing defenses, no measured KGR/KDR figures for the PLKG techniques it discusses, no measured attack success rates for the adversarial-ML taxonomy). The claimed 'unified security framework' in Section V is presented largely as a set of qualitative design insights and an attack taxonomy rather than a concrete, evaluable architecture or protocol.
